coredata.dc:title: Luminescent carbon dots obtained from polymeric waste
coredata.dc:creator: Lauria, Alessandro
Lizundia, Erlantz
coredata.dc:description:
Here a sustainable approach for the bottom-up fabrication of luminescent carbon dots (CDs) through a green synthetic route by using polymeric waste as a single precursor material is developed. Polylactide has been selected as a carbon-containing model polymeric waste because of its remarkable presence in the packaging field and its associated “white-pollution”. Spherical CDs with diameters of 3 nm have been synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal reaction of water-dissolved polylactide oligomers (M
n = 1.200–1.900 g mol−1) at mild temperatures. Chemical, structural, morphological and optical properties of the synthesized CDs have been studied. Results reveal that CDs consist of a carbonaceous nucleus having a sp2 conformation with carboxyl and hydroxyl surface groups, making it dispersible in polar media. The optical features of obtained CDs depend on reaction conditions (time and temperature) and on the insertion of nitrogen groups within the CD structure. A proof of concept study of the application of the proposed approach for the production of luminescent CDs from commercial PLA lids for paper hot cups is also provided. The route here reported provides novel cues for a catalyst-free and organic-solvent-free industrial-scale fabrication of photoluminescent nanomaterials from actual polymeric waste as a viable non-toxic and cheap alternative to quantum dots and rare earth based nanophosphors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrolytic degradation
Polylactide
Upcycling
Carbon-dot
Luminescence
Circular economy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of the method to commercial PLA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physicochemical characterization of starting material and its degradation products: a) proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR); d) Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR); c) differential scanning calorimetry (DSC); d) thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and e) differential TGA (DTGA).

a) FTIR spectra and b) XRD patterns of the degraded PLA (precursor) and CDs obtained after hydrothermal reaction at 240 °C for different times; c) TEM image of CDs synthesized at 240 °C for 4 h (average diameter is reported); d) Detailed HR-TEM image of a CD synthesized at 240 °C for 120 h. FFT patterns of CDs synthesized at 240 °C for 4 h (e) and 120 h (f).

From left to right: photographs under a) natural and b) 365 nm UV illumination of the precursor degraded PLA, water-dispersed CDs obtained after hydrothermal reaction at 240 °C for 20 h, and dried CDs obtained through the same reaction conditions. c) UV–Vis spectra of degraded PLA before and after hydrothermal reaction at 240 °C depending on reaction time. The spectrum of the colloidal dispersion of N-modified CDs is also shown (dashed line). All solutions had a concentration of 0.5 g L−1.

Dependence of the photoluminescence (PL) properties of CDs on hydrothermal reaction a) temperature and b) time. PL (full lines) and PL excitation (PLE) spectra (dashed lines). Insets in a) and b) show the maximum PL intensity as a function of reaction temperature and time, respectively.

a) PL (full lines) and PLE (dashed lines) profiles of bare and N-doped carbon dots synthesized by hydrothermal reaction at 240 °C for 120 h; b) Optical image of the dispersions under UVC illumination (λ = 254 nm). 3D contour plots of the c) undoped and d) N-doped CDs aqueous dispersions.
PLA oligomer extraction upon hydrolytic degradation of commercially available PLA pellets.

head.ce:title: Luminescent carbon dots obtained from polymeric waste

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory for Multifunctional Materials, Department of Materials, ETH Zürich, Vladimir-Prelog-Weg 5, 8093, Zürich, Switzerland

Bilbao Faculty of Engineering. University of the Basque Country (UPV/EHU)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BCMaterials, Basque Center for Materials, Applications and Nanostructures, UPV/EHU Science Park, 48940, Leioa, Spain

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Laboratory for Multifunctional Materials, Department of Materials, ETH Zürich. Vladimir-Prelog-Weg 5, 8093, Zürich, Switzerland.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Here a sustainable approach for the bottom-up fabrication of luminescent carbon dots (CDs) through a green synthetic route by using polymeric waste as a single precursor material is developed. Polylactide has been selected as a carbon-containing model polymeric waste because of its remarkable presence in the packaging field and its associated “white-pollution”. Spherical CDs with diameters of 3 nm have been synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal reaction of water-dissolved polylactide oligomers (M
n = 1.200–1.900 g mol−1) at mild temperatures. Chemical, structural, morphological and optical properties of the synthesized CDs have been studied. Results reveal that CDs consist of a carbonaceous nucleus having a sp2 conformation with carboxyl and hydroxyl surface groups, making it dispersible in polar media. The optical features of obtained CDs depend on reaction conditions (time and temperature) and on the insertion of nitrogen groups within the CD structure. A proof of concept study of the application of the proposed approach for the production of luminescent CDs from commercial PLA lids for paper hot cups is also provided. The route here reported provides novel cues for a catalyst-free and organic-solvent-free industrial-scale fabrication of photoluminescent nanomaterials from actual polymeric waste as a viable non-toxic and cheap alternative to quantum dots and rare earth based nanophosphors.

Luminescent carbon dots (CDs) are fabricated through a green synthetic route by using water and polymeric waste as a single precursor materials.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a metal-free nanomaterial with diameters smaller than 10 nm and stable photoluminescence (PL) properties, have recently arisen as a competitive alternative to other fluorescent materials based on heavy-metal containing quantum dots or rare-earth (RE) doped inorganic nanocrystals (Wang and Hu, 2014). The relevance of CDs at both industrial and scientific level lies on their low toxicity, inherent chemical/photo-physical properties and favourable biocompatibility, which have allowed their use in many practical applications including drug delivery (Sun et al., 2016), catalysis (Li et al., 2010), sensing (Lizundia et al., 2017), optoelectronics (Zhu et al., 2017). These materials also represent an appealing alternative for bioimaging (Teng et al., 2014), lighting technologies, and for energy harvesting in luminescent solar concentrators to improve the efficiency of Si-based photovoltaic technologies (Miao et al., 2015; Villa et al., 2016). In contrast to other semiconducting nanoparticles, the PL of CDs strongly depends on the synthetic conditions, i.e., initial precursor materials and reaction temperature/time, making the modification of their luminescence relatively simple (Medintz et al., 2005).

Although the early works on CD synthesis involved top-down routes with harsh environments and high-temperature solid-state synthetic procedures (laser ablation, arch discharge …) (Teng et al., 2014), soft-solution processing methods, such as hydrothermal synthesis, seem to be more desirable approaches allowing a bottom-up fabrication of CDs at low temperatures and under “greener” conditions (Liu et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2012; Li et al., 2013). Most of the reported works on the synthesis of CDs deal with the use of high-cost or toxic precursors, making their fabrication a not easily scalable process (Baker and Baker, 2010; Qian et al., 2014). In that line, the use of renewable biological by-products as carbon sources has been explored in the last years in the attempt to fabricate CDs through more environment friendly processes. These precursors include the easily accessible grass (Liu et al., 2012), plant leaves (Zhu et al., 2013), apple juice (Mehta et al., 2015), soy milk (Zhu et al., 2012), palm oil (Nasir et al., 2019), wine lees (Varisco et al., 2017) and waste paper (Adolfsson et al., 2015). However, the use of these green materials as precursors does not guarantee the development of an industrial-scale fabrication approach that emerges as an urgent need owing to the growing interest in applications of CDs.

Accordingly, the fabrication of fine chemicals using abundant and accessible precursors under catalyst free and organic solvent free conditions may represent a step forward towards a sustainable society based on circular materials. In this context, several efforts were recently devoted to the upcycling of polymeric waste for the production of value-added products (Pol, V. 2010; Celik et al., 2019). In contrast to recycling, this approach not only aims at the reduction of polymer waste accumulation in the environment with alternative fate with respect to landfills or incineration (Zhuo and Levendis, 2013), but it also holds the promise of the valorisation of waste materials as feedstock resources for fine chemicals or high value functional nanomaterials. Researchers have attempted to produce carbon based nanomaterials from various polymer wastes (Zhuo and Levendis, 2013). Indeed, active carbon (Xu et al., 2018) and molybdenum carbide (Mir and Pandey, 2019) catalysts were produced from polymeric textile wastes. Nevertheless, only few works dealt with the production of fluorescent nanomaterials from biodegradable plastic waste (Aji et al., 2018), involving the support of the reaction with microwave (MW) heating or the use of catalyst, limiting to some extent the easy scalability of such processes.

With a global expected market of USD 2,091.29 million by 2023, polylactide (PLA) is currently the most prominent type of renewable and biodegradable polymer (Mordor Intelligence, 2018). PLA is supplanting petroleum-based commodity plastics in many commercial applications such as flexible packaging, disposable cups or shopping bags. In spite of its well-known biodegradability (Gupta and Kumar, 2007; Madhavan Nampoothiri et al., 2010), its slow degradation in marine environments together with current disposal practices result in the generation of the so-called “white-pollution” (single-use plastic waste) (Schneiderman and Hillmyer, 2017), causing serious environmental issues. Thanks to the potentially hydrolysable ester bonds in PLA chains, this polymer can be easily fragmented down to its constituent oligomers by hydrolytic degradation reactions (Larrañaga and Lizundia, 2019). Therefore, such oligomers resulting from PLA waste could be used as a carbon-containing feedstock platform for the fabrication of photoluminescent carbon nanomaterials.

Here we report on the fabrication of CDs through a green synthetic route using polymeric waste as a single precursor material. Polylactide has been used as a carbon source and model material because of its ubiquitous presence in the packaging field. A one-pot hydrothermal synthesis was used to convert polylactide oligomers obtained from the hydrolytic degradation of PLA into photoluminescent carbon nanomaterials. The physico-chemical and morphological properties of synthesized CDs were studied depending on reaction conditions. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra for different hydrothermal temperatures and times were also studied. The novel synthetic approach here reported provides cues for the fabrication of photoluminescent carbonaceous nanomaterials from polymeric waste as a viable non-toxic and cheap alternative to RE-doped fluorescent nanoparticles and quantum dots.

Poly(lactide) (PLA) pellets supplied by Total Corbion PLA (The Netherlands) were used as starting material for carbon dot synthesis. 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA, 97%, 369519-100G) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

Firstly, polylactide was hydrolytically degraded by immersing 1 g of pellets in 100 mL of deionized water at 95 °C and magnetically stirring (300 rpm) the dispersion for 3 weeks. As depicted in Scheme 1 , after the first week PLA pellets are degraded down into microplastics, i.e. small plastic pieces below 5 mm, which become water soluble after 2 weeks as a result of the PLA chain-shortening. After 3 weeks of hydrolytic degradation, the pH of the medium drops down to ∼2.3 due to the release of the acidic by-products during PLA chain-scission (Hoüglund et al., 2012).

CDs were then synthesized by a bottom-up approach through hydrothermal reactions using degraded PLA oligomers as a direct source (water-soluble PLA oligomers from the hydrolytic degradation was used with no further purification) (Lim et al., 2014; Sevilla and Fuertes, 2009). In a typical procedure, 10 mL of the H2O dispersion containing degraded PLA oligomers at a concentration of 0.1 mg mL−1 were vigorously stirred for 30 min and were then transferred into Teflon-lined autoclaves. The reaction was carried out in an oven at temperatures ranging from 180 to 240 °C (±0.5 °C) for different times. Then, the autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature naturally and the resulting product was filtered using a 0.22 μm pore-size PVDF filter membrane (Millex®-HV). After the reaction and complete cooling, white dispersions turned transparent to the naked eye, indicating the conversion of the precursor into CDs. Aqueous CD dispersions were stored at 4 °C (±1 °C) until needed.

Similarly, nitrogen-doped CDs (N-doped CDs) were obtained upon hydrothermal reaction of the H2O dispersion having PLA oligomers in the presence of TTDDA according to the approach proposed by Liu et al. (2011). 0.2 mg of TTDDA were incorporated into the water-dissolved PLA oligomers so to ensure a PLA:TTDDA weight ratio of 5:1. By stirring for 30 min a homogeneous solution was obtained, which was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave. The same process described for the synthesis of non-doped CDs was then applied.

Molecular weight distribution of samples was determined using a Malvern Viscotek GPCmax gel permeation chromatograph (GPC). Solid samples (obtained after drying the aqueous PLA oligomer dispersion) were dissolved in DMF containing 1 g L−1 of LiBr at a concentration of 1 mg mL−1. Measurements were done at 318 K at a flow rate of 1 mL min−1. Calibration was obtained using narrowly-distributed polystyrene standards. Proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker AV300 MHz instrument at room temperature using deuterated chloroform (CDCl3) as solvent. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) measurements were performed on a Bruker Alpha FT-IR Spectrometer equipped with diamond ATR optics. The thermal behaviour of starting materials and CDs was determined by using a Mettler Toledo DSC 822e calorimeter (temperature accuracy: ± 0.2 °C; temperature precision: ± 0.02 °C) under nitrogen atmosphere (50 mL min−1). Samples were sealed in an aluminium pan and were heated from −20 to 190 °C at a rate of 10 °C·min−1 in order to determine the glass transition and melting temperatures and enthalpies (T
g and T
m ). The instrument is routinely calibrated against indium standards and measurements were performed under a nitrogen blanket. Thermal degradation behaviour of starting materials and CDs was studied by thermal gravimetric analysis in a Mettler Toledo TGA/SDTA851e (temperature accuracy: ± 0.25 °C; weighing accuracy: 0.005%) using alumina crucibles under nitrogen atmosphere by heating the samples from room temperature to 600 °C at 10 °C·min−1. Before their characterization, the samples were dried at 60 °C under vacuum for 24 h. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using a PANalytical Empyrean powder diffractometer in reflection mode using Cu Kα radiation and operating at 45 kV and 40 mA to investigate the crystalline structure of synthesized P-dots. Samples were mounted on a zero background silicon sample holder. Data were collected from 5 to 50° 2θ (step size = 0.026°) at RT. The morphology of synthesized CDs was addressed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using an X-FEG FEI Talos instrument. A droplet of CDs in water suspension (0.01% (w/w)) was deposited onto a graphene coated TEM copper grid.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra on aqueous dispersions containing synthesized CDs (0.1 mg mL−1) were recorded with a JASCO V-660 spectrometer operated in the range 200–800 nm with a bandwidth of 1 nm and spectral resolution of 0.2 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were recorded on a Jasco FP8500 fluorimeter with emission and excitation bandwidths of 1 nm and spectral resolution of 1 nm. 3D spectra were recorded in emission mode by scanning excitation every 5 nm in the range 200–500 nm. All spectra were corrected by the excitation Xe lamp spectrum and detector spectral response.

Firstly, PLA was converted down to its constituent oligomers through a controlled hydrolytic degradation, where H2O molecules attack the ester linkages on the polylactide backbone. Although the study was carried out using PLA from commercial pellets, PLA oligomers can also be obtained from commercially available lids (see Fig. S1). Proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) was firstly used to assess the structure of the low molecular weight oligomers obtained after the hydrolytic degradation of commercial PLA pellets. Accordingly, Fig. 1 a shows representative 1H NMR spectra of initial materials together with the oligomers obtained after their controlled degradation. Overall, it is observed that non-degraded PLA displays a lower amount of peaks than its degraded counterpart. The characteristic doublet at 1.58 and 1.60 ppm due to the methyl group protons (aH) and the quadruplet at 5.15, 5.17, 5.19 and 5.21 ppm arising from the proton of the methine group (bH) of the main chain, are observed for both samples (Llorens et al., 2014). On the contrary, the intensity of the signals located at 1.49–1.51, 3.04 and 4.34–4.39 ppm assigned to the methyl (cH), hydroxyl (dH) and methine (eH) of the PLA end-groups respectively is higher for the degraded polylactide, indicating that the molecular conformation changes during hydrolytic degradation (Kameno et al., 2016; Xiao et al., 2011). Accordingly, obtained 1H NMR spectra (see Fig. S2 for the enlarged 1H NMR spectra showing the specific signals of interest) could be used for molecular weight determination as:
(1)
M n
= 90 + 72
m e t h i n e
p r o t o n
s i g n a l
α − m e t h i n e
p r o t o n
s i g n a l
where 90 and 72 are the molecular weights of the monomer and repeating unit respectively and α-methine represent the methane groups nearby the terminal –OH groups (signal at 4.34–4.39 ppm) (Codari et al., 2010). A number-average molecular weight of 1145 g mol−1 has been obtained confirming that upon degradation macromolecules undergo chain-scission reactions, yielding a final product (oligomers) with more numerous end-groups, i.e. shorter chains are developed. GPC curves (not shown) indicate a M
n of 1890 g mol−1 for degraded pellets, which is in line with NMR results.

The chain shortening induced by the hydrolytic degradation of PLA induces a higher presence of carboxylic acid and alcohol groups per unit mass in comparison with non-degraded material. Therefore, the relative ratio between the ester bonds and end functional groups, i.e., carboxylic acid/alcohol groups, could be used as an indicator of the extent of the degradation process. In that sense, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra of raw PLA pellet and degraded PLA normalized to the characteristic carbonyl absorption band located at 1759 cm−1 are shown in Fig. 1b (for the sake of clarity only the 2000-600 cm−1 region is shown). A marked chain shortening is suggested for degraded PLA as the intensity of the –CO stretching at 1268 cm−1 is lower for the degraded PLA, indicating a reduction on the amount of ester bonds (Achmad et al., 2009). Similarly, Fig. 1c–e show the thermal properties of raw PLA and degraded material. According to the DSC curves in Fig. 1c, commercial PLA pellets display a heat enthalpy change at 72.2 °C as a result of the glass transition (T
g ), which is followed by an endothermic melting event centred at 180.9 °C respectively (T
m ). In contrast, degraded PLA pellets only display a melting event at 160.2 °C, indicating the presence of shorter chains with increased mobility and thus lower T
m (Lizundia et al., 2015).

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), shown in Fig. 1d and e, also confirms the molecular-weight decrease induced by hydrolytic degradation already shown by GPC, 1H NMR, FTIR and DSC, where the thermal degradation of degraded PLA onsets at lower temperatures as a result of the lower thermal stability of short chains (Lizundia et al., 2015).

Hydrothermal reactions have been proven an efficient approach to synthesize carbonaceous materials with photoluminescent properties from several renewable materials such as natural saccharides (Sevilla and Fuertes, 2009; Wang and Hu, 2014). Inspired by those reports, here we use PLA oligomers obtained from polymeric waste as a green precursor to develop carbon nanomaterials with potential photoluminescent properties. Accordingly, CDs were synthesized via a bottom-up approach through hydrothermal reaction at temperatures ranging from 180 to 240 °C and for times extending up to 120 h. Firstly, the pH value and Zeta-potential of CD dispersions was measured and compared with the source PLA oligomer solution. It was found that for a reaction temperature of 240 °C, an increase of the reaction time from 4 to 120 h entails a pH rise from 2.3 to nearly 2.7 and a Zeta-potential increase from −17.4 ± 0.1 to 4.4 ± 1.3 mV, indicating that the acidic water-soluble PLA oligomers are being consumed to yield non-acidic products (Hoüglund et al., 2012). In fact, the higher the presence of non-acidic products, the higher the pH and the less the material is negatively charged.

FTIR has been carried out to study the functional groups present in the obtained materials. Fig. 2 a shows the FTIR spectra of CDs synthesized at 240 °C for 4, 20 and 120 h, where the spectrum corresponding to the PLA oligomers (the source material used for hydrothermal reactions) is shown for comparison. A very broad absorption band at 3600-2400 cm−1 arising from the O–H stretching in CDs is observed for all the reactions times, which is absent in the case of PLA oligomer. The characteristic stretching of the carbonyl group (CO) of PLA, which is centred at 1759 cm−1 (Meaurio et al., 2009), is continuously shifted towards lower wave numbers with increasing reaction time (from 1717 to 1706 after 4 and 120 h of reaction respectively), suggesting a possible molecular weight increase of the resulting material. At the same time, the shoulder at 1562 and the narrow band at 1025 cm−1 are be ascribed to the CC stretch of sp2 hybridized carbon and surface –COO- groups respectively (Yang et al., 2015, 2014).

These results agree well with the carbon dot formation mechanism, where it is reported that CDs grow from initially formed carbonaceous nuclei (sp2 conformation) that is achieved thanks to the onset of PLA oligomer dehydration/fragmentation during early stages of the hydrothermal reaction. Then, these oligomers are able to polymerize and condense onto the surface of carbonaceous nuclei by diffusion reactions, yielding materials covered by carboxyl and hydroxyl groups (Sevilla and Fuertes, 2009; Yang et al., 2015). The presence of such hydrophilic groups with abundant oxygen atoms render CDs highly dispersible in industrially relevant solvents such as water or acetone, where no appreciable CD agglomeration was observed even after 3 months of storage.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) has been carried out to understand the changes occurring on the crystalline structure upon hydrothermal reaction. As shown in Fig. 2b, PLA oligomers display a pattern characterized by a narrow and strong diffraction peak at 2θ = 16.5° together with weaker peaks at 2θ = 14.8, 19.0 and 22.3°. These features indicate the presence of the high order α crystalline form of PLA (Lizundia et al., 2016; Wasanasuk et al., 2011), consisting of an orthorhombic unit cell having a 103 helical conformation (ICDD card No. 00-043-1858 using PDF4 database). On the contrary, XRD patterns of CDs are characterized by a broad band asymmetrically centred at 2θ = 20.1°. This broad diffraction peak may be ascribed to the (002) diffraction peak of graphite and indicates a predominantly amorphous disordered structure in CDs, consistent with previous reports (Chen et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2017). It is also observed that the reaction temperature barely changes the crystalline features of CDs since all 3 patterns remain similar. The morphology of CDs synthesized at 240 °C for 4 h has been assessed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and is shown in Fig. 2c. TEM imaging shows well-dispersed spherical tiny nanoparticles with a diameter of 2.99 ± 0.57 nm (statistics based on count of 60 particles), which agrees well with previously reported data on CDs (Wang and Hu, 2014).

High resolution TEM (HRTEM) measurements provide further details about the morphology of CDs. It is observed in Fig. 2d that a CD synthesized at 240 °C for 120 h present a structurally ordered nature at the nanoscale, where crystalline fringes with a regular lattice spacing of 0.23 nm are observed. Such interplanar spacing agrees well with the (102) diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon, further confirming the presence of such species within the CD core (Jia et al., 2012). In order to get further insights on the crystalline character of CDs, we performed Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) on CDs that have been synthesized at 240 °C for 4 and 120 h (see the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) in Figs. S3a–b). Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) in Fig. S3c shows the elemental composition within the area of interest, confirming that the selected FFT areas are solely formed by carbon and oxygen (Cu and Si arise from the sample substrate). It is evident in Fig. 2e that CDs obtained after 4 h display weak and broad diffuse rings, confirming its poor crystalline character. On the contrary, after 120 h of hydrothermal reaction (Fig. 2f) the rings become more marked and 6 spots displaying a short-range order similar to graphite are observed, indicating that CDs develop a more marked crystalline structure.

Overall, we found a pivotal role of the molecular weight of the precursor on the PL of the resulting material. When the same synthetic conditions were applied to water-soluble lactic acid monomers or chains having M
n = 5.000 g mol−1 no photoluminescent response was found when excited by UV light, suggesting that few repeating units (10–20) are required to activate significant luminescence in the materials. Additional control experiments using microwave synthesis were also carried out to confirm the suitability of polylactide oligomers as source material for CDs formation. Unfortunately, we found that microwave fails to provide photoluminescent CDs, indicating that apart from reaction temperatures in the range of 180–240 °C, high pressures are also required for the formation of optically active CDs when using polylactide oligomers as a source material. Moreover, it is important to note that as a result of the tiny size of CDs, we fail to purify the product obtained after hydrothermal reaction by dialysis since CDs pass through the small holes of the membrane. Therefore, we expect the presence of some non-reacted polylactide oligomers and small molecular weight reaction products. We are aware that this issue needs to be solved before any potential application of the CDs here obtained.

The optical characterization of degraded PLA before and after hydrothermal reaction was used in order to monitor the precursor decomposition and the formation of luminescent carbon dots. Fig. 3 shows the appearance and absorption profiles of dispersions depending on the hydrothermal reaction. The precursor dispersions appear as white milky suspensions (Fig. 3a and b), as confirmed by the higher baseline in the absorption profile (Fig. 3c). After hydrothermal treatment, the dispersion becomes clear and a blue emission stimulated by UV light is activated, confirming the substantial conversion of the precursor polymer into optically active species. Once dried completely, the obtained white powder preserves the UV-stimulated optical activity.

The progressive conversion of the precursor into carbonaceous particles is confirmed by the evolution of the absorption profiles (Fig. 3c). Indeed the hydrothermal reaction induces the onset of absorption bands in the UV range, namely at around 240 nm and 280 nm. Both bands are not visible after reaction at 240 °C for only 4 h, while they become more and more evident as the reaction time is increased. The stronger UV–Vis absorption at 247 nm ascribed to π-electrons in the sp2 hybridized regions (Yuan et al., 2017), while the shoulder at around 280 nm is associated to π–π∗ transitions of the aromatic sp2 carbons within the C-dot core (Dong et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2018). Both bands rise with the reaction time, suggesting that both carbon conformations are present in our materials. The modification of the reaction mixture with 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA) induces a slight yellow tint to the obtained clear water dispersions, due to the substantial change in the absorption profile. Two new bands at lower energies are observed, likely resulting from the incorporation of N-containing polyaromatic structures into the carbon nanodot core (Qu et al., 2014).

The photoluminescence 3D plots of the obtained materials were recorded in order to characterize the fluorescence properties of the synthesized carbon dots (see the PL of water-dispersed CDs obtained after hydrothermal reaction at 240 °C for different times and upon N-doping in Fig. S4 and the photoluminescence excitation contour maps for different reaction temperature and times in Fig. S5). All water dispersions after hydrothermal reaction, exhibit a blue emission centred at around 400 nm, with a single excitation band in the 290–320 nm range, irrespective of reaction conditions. Interestingly, we do not observe a strong dependence of the emission on the excitation wavelength (Sun et al., 2006). Fig. 4 displays photoluminescence-photoluminescence excitation (PL-PLE) curves for the hydrothermally treated water dispersions, depending on reaction temperature and time (Fig. 4a and b, respectively). By reacting the mixtures at higher temperatures, the excitation and emission profiles slightly change (Fig. 4a), as confirmed by the 10 nm red shift and broadening of the emission of CDs synthesized at the highest temperature (240 °C) compared to lower temperatures. The light output, expressed as maximum PL intensity, increases with reaction temperature, suggesting the incomplete conversion of the polymeric precursor into CDs at lower temperatures. Indeed, mixtures reacted at the highest temperature showed the highest fluorescence (Fig. 4a, inset).

The role of reaction time on the properties of the materials is displayed in Fig. 4b. Again, the emission only slightly depends on the reaction conditions. The gradual redshift of the excitation maximum cannot be easily explained, although it might reflect the higher self-absorption of the dispersion, considering the higher absorption of dispersions reacted for longer times in the range 200–300 nm (Fig. 3c). The emission intensity (inset in Fig. 4b), on the contrary, strongly depends on reaction time. This evidence points at the gradual progression of the reaction toward crystalline carbon-based nanoparticles, depending on both temperature and time of reaction, in agreement with structural and optical analyses discussed above.

The modification of the reaction mixture through the addition of TTDDA imparts a substantial change of the electronic structure and therefore on the spectral features of the obtained materials. Fig. 5 displays the comparison of the PL-PLE features of bare CDs and N-doped CDs obtained by hydrothermal reaction at 240 °C for 120 h. Indeed the insertion of nitrogen groups in the structure leads to a red shift, toward the visible range, of both excitation an emission bands. N-doped CDs are mostly excited in the UVA spectral region (370 nm) while the bare CDs are mostly activated only when illuminated with UVC (318 nm) radiation. The emission also shifts from 398 nm to 441 nm (full width at half maximum, FWHM, remains unchanged at 67 nm), with an additional band in the green range (500–520 nm), as also observed by other researchers for N-doped CDs (Sciortino et al., 2018, 2017). These results are of particular importance for applications in which the luminescence stimulation by UVA radiation is preferred, like in bioimaging or light harvesting in photovoltaic luminescent solar concentrators (Miao et al., 2015; Villa et al., 2016). This difference is also well evidenced by the green tint of the emission of N-modified dots excited by 254 nm UV radiation (Fig. 5b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the method to commercial PLA
ce:section.ce:para: Remarkably, the approach here proposed can also be extended to actual polymeric waste. Due to the great relevance of the packaging field in the development of biodegradable polymers such as PLA, a commercial PLA lid was considered as a potential carbon source for CDs synthesis, as a proof of concept. PLA oligomers were extracted after a controlled hydrolytic degradation of the commercial white Ingeo™ compostable lid for paper hot cups (Fig. S6a). The physicochemical and thermal properties of the obtained oligomers are similar to those obtained from PLA pellets (Figs. S6b and c), despite the presence of additives aimed at conferring higher mechanical and thermal resistance to the lid. TEM analysis (Fig. S6d) shows that carbon dots are also obtained upon hydrothermal reaction of degraded lid, also displaying fluorescence features similar to those of CDs obtained from PLA pellets. These features suggest the applicability of the proposed approach to the upcycling of degradable polymeric waste of the packaging industry derived from commercial PLA (like food containers, plastic films and disposable lids for paper cups) into functional luminescent CDs.

In this work we show for the first time a synthetic route for the fabrication of photoluminescent carbon dots using commonly found PLA polymeric waste as a carbon-containing platform building block. The bottom-up synthesis here reported is simple, catalyst and organic solvent-free and uses a readily-available precursor, making it an industrially viable alternative. Uniform and spherical CDs of 3 nm in diameter have been obtained upon hydrothermal reaction of water-soluble polylactide oligomers with a number of repeating units in the range of 10–20. Although CDs were predominantly amorphous at the macroscale, results reveal that a short-range structural order can be achieved at longer reactions times (or higher temperatures), which in turn influences the PL response of the material. Overall, a blue emission centred at 400 nm was obtained when exciting the CDs (either in solution or dried state) by UV light. The emission can be easily red shifted down to the blue-green range by modifying the dots with nitrogen doping, which also leads to a maximum of excitation red shifted in the near UV. This confirms their possible use as rare-earth-free, environmentally friendly, and cheap phosphor nano-systems. The synthetic procedure reported, which can be easily extended to the upcycling of commercial compostable lids for paper hot cups as starting material, holds a great potential for the development of CDs by a green, industrially scalable fabrication method, which currently emerges as an urgent need owing to the growing use of fluorescent nanoparticles in many practical fields.

Alessandro Lauria: Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Resources, Visualization, Funding acquisition. Erlantz Lizundia: Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to ETH Zurich for financial support. Dr. Elena Tervoort and ScopeM are kindly acknowledged for TEM analysis.
